body.brief: Write a precise spec (max 220 words, plain prose, no headings) for a parking garage fee calculator function with these rules: the first 15 minutes are free; after that, 3.50 dollars per started hour; daily cap 24.00 dollars; on weekends the daily cap is 18.00 dollars instead; a lost ticket costs a flat 45 dollars regardless of duration; electric vehicles get a 20 percent discount on the pre-cap total, rounded to the nearest 0.25 dollars. State inputs, output, and every rule.

spec.systemPrompt: [role:speccer] You write concise, precise software specs. Output ONLY the spec text — no preamble, no headings, no markdown.
[role:coder] You are a coder. You receive a natural-language spec and output ONLY TypeScript code implementing it (max 80 lines, minimal comments). No explanation, no markdown fences.

[role:respeccer] You are a reverse-engineer. You receive source code and output ONLY a natural-language spec (max 220 words, plain prose) describing exactly what the code does. You have never seen any earlier spec. No preamble, no markdown.

body.text:
A parking garage fee calculator function accepts four inputs: duration in minutes (integer, non-negative), is_weekend (boolean), is_electric (boolean), and is_lost_ticket (boolean). It returns the fee as a decimal number rounded to the nearest cent.

For lost tickets, return 45 dollars flat, ignoring all other inputs.

For regular tickets, calculate as follows. If duration is 15 minutes or less, the fee is zero. Otherwise, round duration up to the nearest whole hour, multiply by 3.50 dollars to get the pre-cap total, then apply the electric vehicle discount: if is_electric is true, multiply the pre-cap total by 0.80 and round the result to the nearest 0.25 dollars. Determine the daily cap: 24.00 dollars on weekdays, 18.00 dollars on weekends. Cap the fee at the daily cap—take the minimum of the discounted fee and the cap. Round the final result to the nearest cent and return it.

Example: 90 minutes on a weekday, non-electric, not lost. Round up to 2 hours: 2 × 3.50 = 7.00 dollars. No discount. Daily cap is 24.00 dollars. Final fee: 7.00 dollars. Example: 200 minutes on a weekend, electric, not lost. Round up to 4 hours: 4 × 3.50 = 14.00 dollars. Apply 20 percent discount: 14.00 × 0.80 = 11.20, round to nearest 0.25: 11.25 dollars. Daily cap is 18.00 dollars. Final fee: 11.25 dollars.

The function calculates parking fees based on duration and vehicle type. If a lost ticket is reported, it returns a flat $45 penalty. Otherwise, if parking duration is 15 minutes or less, it's free. For longer stays, the fee is $3.50 per hour, rounded up from the total minutes. Electric vehicles receive a 20% discount, rounded to the nearest quarter. The final fee is capped at a daily maximum of $18 on weekends or $24 on weekdays. All monetary values are rounded to the nearest cent before returning.

This function calculates parking fees based on duration, vehicle type, day type, and ticket status. If a ticket is lost, it charges a flat $45 fee. For valid tickets, the first 15 minutes are free. Beyond that, the function rounds up to the nearest hour and charges $3.50 per hour. Electric vehicles receive a 20% discount, rounded to the nearest quarter dollar. The total fee is capped at a daily maximum of $24 on weekdays or $18 on weekends. The final result is rounded to the nearest cent.

This function calculates parking fees based on duration and vehicle characteristics. If a ticket is marked lost, it returns a flat $45 fee. Otherwise, the first 15 minutes are free, and any remaining time rounds up to the nearest hour at $3.50 per hour. Electric vehicles receive a 20% discount, with the result rounded to the nearest quarter dollar. The final fee is capped at a daily maximum of $24 on weekdays or $18 on weekends. All fees are rounded to the nearest cent.

The function calculates parking fees based on duration and vehicle type. Lost tickets incur a flat $45 charge. Otherwise, the first 15 minutes are free. Chargeable time is rounded up to the nearest hour and multiplied by $3.50/hour. Electric vehicles receive a 20% discount on the calculated fee. The result is rounded to the nearest quarter dollar. Weekend (Saturday-Sunday) parking has a $18 daily cap, while weekdays cap at $24. The final fee is returned rounded to cents.

The function calculates parking fees based on entry and exit times, vehicle type, and lost ticket status. If a ticket is lost, it returns a flat $45 fee. Otherwise, it computes the duration between entry and exit times in minutes. The first 15 minutes are free; beyond that, time is rounded up to the nearest hour and charged at $3.50 per hour. Electric vehicles receive a 20% discount. The calculated fee is then rounded to the nearest quarter-dollar. Finally, a daily cap is applied: $24 on weekdays or $18 on weekends (Saturday and Sunday). The function returns the final fee rounded to the nearest cent.

A parking fee calculator that charges based on duration, vehicle type, and day of week. Lost tickets incur a flat $45 fee. For valid tickets, the first 15 minutes are free. Beyond that, fees are calculated at $3.50 per hour (rounded up), with a 20% discount for electric vehicles. Fees are rounded to the nearest quarter dollar. Daily caps apply: $24 on weekdays, $18 on weekends. The final fee is capped at these daily maximums and rounded to two decimal places. The function accepts entry and exit times as Date objects, vehicle type (standard or electric), a lost ticket flag, and day of week (0–6, where 0 and 6 are weekend days).

The function calculates parking fees based on entry and exit times, vehicle type, and day of week. If a lost ticket is reported, it returns a flat $45 fee. Otherwise, it calculates billable time by subtracting a 15-minute grace period from the total duration and rounding up to the nearest hour. The base hourly rate is $3.50 per hour. Electric vehicles receive a 20% discount. Fees are rounded to the nearest quarter-dollar. A daily cap applies based on the day: $24 on weekdays and $18 on weekends. The final result is rounded to the nearest cent.

A parking fee calculator that determines charges based on vehicle stay duration and type. Lost tickets incur a flat $45 fee. Otherwise, the system subtracts a 15-minute grace period from the total stay, rounds up to the nearest hour, and charges $3.50 per hour. Electric vehicles receive a 20% discount on the hourly rate. The calculated fee is rounded to the nearest quarter-dollar, then capped at $24 for weekdays or $18 for weekends, with the final amount rounded to the nearest cent. The function accepts entry and exit times as Date objects, vehicle type (regular or electric), day of week (0–6), and a lost ticket flag, returning the fee as a number.

The function calculates parking fees based on duration and vehicle type. If a lost ticket is reported, it returns a flat $45 fee. Otherwise, it computes billable time by subtracting a 15-minute grace period from the total stay duration. The billable minutes are rounded up to the nearest hour and multiplied by an hourly rate of $3.50, reduced to $2.80 for electric vehicles. The fee is rounded to the nearest quarter-dollar, then capped at a daily maximum: $24 on weekdays or $18 on weekends. Finally, the result is rounded to the nearest cent.

This code calculates parking fees by delegating to Claude via API, then verifies the result locally. It accepts parking duration in minutes, vehicle type (standard or electric), lost-ticket status, and day of week. The function applies a tiered fee structure: lost tickets incur a flat $45 charge; otherwise it subtracts a 15-minute grace period, rounds billable time up to the nearest hour, applies hourly rates ($3.50 standard, $2.80 electric), rounds to the nearest quarter-dollar, applies a daily cap ($24 weekdays, $18 weekends), and returns the result rounded to the nearest cent. The Claude call asks the model to compute the fee following these exact rules and return only the numeric value. The local verification recalculates using the same logic, ensuring consistency. The main function runs four test cases covering different scenarios: short standard parking on a weekday, two-hour electric on a weekday, lost ticket on weekend, and eight-hour standard on weekend, logging results to console.
